While disguised as Blood Legion, the Flame Legion plans to sabotage Iron Legion's new siege engine. Samona Ironclaw is standing by to help you prevent the Flame Legion from turning Iron against Blood.

While earning your place with the Rift Hunters, head out to explore the islands of Skywatch Archipelago.

While envoys have reached various groups of quaggan and largos, it's hard to determine the impact that the death of the water dragon has had on their cultures or the sea as a whole. The largos have always been difficult to reach out to, and for now, at least, the alliance has been informed that the Tethyos Houses are not open to cooperation. The ward has at least one largos within its ranks who has expressed an eagerness to support this cause. It may be worth considering sending one of their own out to see if she can make better headway.    We'd hoped to have better luck with the deep-sea quaggan, but our envoys were rebuffed once again. Truthfully, this is unexpected for that species; they are usually quite eager to volunteer themselves to larger causes. Whatever is going on down there must be quite serious and should not be ignored.    Some asura from the colleges, ward members, and engineers from Xunlai Jade have proposed an initiative to outfit the Pact's submarines with better tech and magic, but they seem to be busier arguing with each other than making legitimate progress on that front. Alliance intervention is recommended.

While I am labeling these field reports with 90%% certainty as to the accuracy of the numbering, I do recognize the optimism one must apply to view these as "field reports." I do what I must to stave off the further erosion of my spirits.    After escaping the "rabble"—wait, I suppose I should cover that first.    Ignaxious's assertion that they would find me adequate dinner fare was thankfully erroneous. Embarrassingly, I believe they even mistook me for one of their kind at first. From a distance, assuredly, as this seems an impossible (and highly offensive) mix-up to me. But it is one that I'm grateful for, nonetheless.    So, after cleaning myself of slobber and engaging in polite—if hard to follow—conversation, I skirted my way along the land's edge in an attempt to steer clear of both physical and mental probes and continue my mission of gathering intel. My hope is that if and when the Lady Peitha's invasion begins, these field reports will be discovered and put to some use.    Granted, they've only served to document my own self-centered tribulations thus far. Given that they will likely end up as low Kryptis excrement, my disappointment in myself is less than it might otherwise be.    —Operative Selly

While I can see the cruelty behind using chak as a resource, the advancements they've been able to make here *should* benefit, *could* benefit, so many people. I can't expose my bias, though. The Inquest is still a thing, and if any of this is going to fall apart it might as well start with them. It always does, after all. It's just disappointing to live in a world where something that can benefit a lot of people can cause harm... But maybe I'm being naive. That's just how the world works, right? Feeling my morality a bit, is all...  The Rata Novans seem to have things under control, but for how much longer? We haven't seen how the Kryptis will affect this place, yet, but I can't imagine it going well. It never does.  After we wrap things up here for the day, Dagda is taking me with her to the observatory. Another fractal, tucked away in the islands. Apparently she and Waiting Sorrow used it as research facility... I'm just happy Dagda is bringing me along. I'm feeling more helpful now than I have in years. Like I'm a part of something bigger than me, which would have threatened me years ago. But, now... It's different.

While I rarely leave the confines of my domain, I possess the finest and most robust collection of trinkets from around the world. Adventurers travel across Tyria, finding items of all sorts from locations multifarious, and toss them into my forge in the hopes of receiving something better in return.Most of the time, the skritt handle the method of compensation. I have to imagine that's working out just fine, considering I've yet to receive any complaints that I've read.Still, I admit that this system is not entirely perfect. For example, the wizard Isgarren came to my forge long ago seeking special compounds that I imagine were used for some magical purpose or another. I heard of this after the fact, and was most curious what one of his stature might want to get out of my collection! But upon closer investigation, I realized with a hint of despair that there was no record of what exactly Isgarren tossed into my forge, nor what he received in return, as the skritt are rather poor at maintaining proper documentation.Also, their handwriting is quite terrible.This has resulted in a much more robust plan to eventually implement a system that will record all of the items entering and exiting the forge. We've yet to put the plan into action, of course, as I like to give myself lots of time to stew over new ideas for the Mystic Forge operation before putting them in place. It's been a while, admittedly, but great ideas take time to perfect, and I have a lot of great ideas.I maintain hope that someday that curious wizard will return with need of my services. Should the situation arise, I plan to inquire at length about the nature of his work and just where he has been all this time. But, until then, all I can do is have Miyani keep an ear to the ground. It has been a very long time since Isgarren has been seen or heard from, so I have my concerns.
While I remain skeptical of your "White Mantle" and some of your promised intentions should Kryta return to your rule, I agree that the time to act is upon us.

While much has been made of the seers' connection to magic, the theory that they possessed a once-great civilization that has since been lost appears to be entirely baseless. While the proponents are quick to point out the destructive capabilities of the mursaat for explaining the lack of surviving architecture, they miss the more obvious explanation: the seers never built them in the first place. In all likelihood, they were a primarily nomadic people, possibly early agrarian in some places, but there just exists very little evidence to suggest more than that.    Supporters of this idea also frequently reference the oft-repeated legends of the seers and their influence in the ancient war against the Elder Dragons. What they fail to recognize is that this fits in perfectly with one of the most common archetypal myths: the ancient, powerful civilization, who once ruled over the world, but then fell and left it to us to take over. It's entirely possible that the seers played a role in the conflict, but that doesn't mean these myths can be taken as literal truth.    If this field of study is going to experience any success, we need to avoid expending our efforts searching for evidence we're unlikely to ever find. While not technically impossible, the likelihood of discovering a massive, lost seer structure is indistinguishable from zero. It would be more sensible for us to focus our efforts on better understanding what we've already uncovered.

While rescuing Tegwen from the krait, I also helped an asura named Larra. It was fortunate she was there, because she saw the skritt making off with the Orrian mirror. She's promised to help us retrieve it from the rat-creatures.

While Rox and Braham tended to the survivors, I made my way through the rest of the maze—finally confronting Scarlet at the apex of the arena. Before our fight began, she threatened me if I didn't stop meddling in her plans. As our weapons clashed, she utilized every trick available in that arena. She almost succumbed, but Scarlet knew when to pull back. She stared me down, noting my role in foiling her operation before fleeing the city for her own safety.Scarlet keeps a short list of people who've gotten in her way, and guess who just earned their place at the top.

While Taimi continued her research at the ley line hub, Rox, Braham, and I investigated the waypoint disruption at Concordia. We found the fort ravaged by jungle tendrils and other creatures. I led the search party that rescued Lindsay and some of her colleagues before fending off the remaining attackers at Goldenlight Hallow lab. Tactician Art mentioned that the vines started sprouting from the ground shortly after a shipment of magical artifacts arrived. I'm not sure of the connection there.

Rox made an offhand comment about Taimi. I think the kid is getting on her nerves.

Why then, do I leave behind these pages, detailing the banishment of our former compatriot? Why detail the very curse that would, if one were to find and counter the spell, nullify the transaction that cost Isgarren so dearly?    The truth, dear reader, is this: my people are not without humility. Our wisdom and our guidance are prone to fallacy. As I was taken by my anger, I knew that it was entirely possible that, even in his exile, Isgarren could outlast the many perils of this world and survive us all. After all, I know him better than any—there was a version of our society where he could have risen to doyen.  In that case, what good is our judgment if we have all dissolved to dust?      If you are reading this, and you have made common cause with him, then perhaps we must face the reality that his way was, in fact, the correct path. If he has lived long and well enough to inspire those he gathers to ally with him, to become his friend, to risk all on his behalf, and return to this sacred place in search of a way to unravel our curse...perhaps he has earned such a stay.    You, who would call Isgarren friend, you will be the ones who truly seal his fate. You should have enough information from these pages to know what to do. I only ask you choose wisely.
